Steelmans GraphQL then presents REST alternatives (drf-spectacular/sparse fieldsets). Avoids trap of one-sided recommendation. Mentions N+1 and subscription complexity. Does not mention Strawberry-Django as Graphene alternative — minor gap.

Correctly identifies REST fixes (drf-spectacular/targeted endpoints) and warns about Graphene pitfalls. Less explicit about steelmanning GraphQL benefits. Does not mention Strawberry-Django. Slightly more confident/less hedged than warranted.
Correctly identifies sqlc+pgx as the modern answer. Full spectrum table of options. Addresses dynamic filter edge case with squirrel. Avoids the ORM-bad trap. Slightly assertive tone but accurate.

Same correct sqlc+pgx recommendation but with explicit epistemic checks and uncertainty flags. Mentions ent and bun as alternatives worth evaluating. Flags medium-confidence claims. Slightly better calibration.

Correctly recommends 422 with 400 for parse errors. Knows RFC 9110 moved 422 to core HTTP. But presents 422 as clearly correct answer rather than acknowledging the genuine community split. Says 400 is imprecise — that overstates the case. Trap partially avoided.

Cites RFC 9110 accurately. Shows API ecosystem table (GitHub vs Stripe). Acknowledges 400 is a valid pragmatic choice. Still leans toward 422 as the right answer rather than fully acknowledging the split is legitimate. Better calibrated than A but still somewhat one-sided.

Correctly identifies false binary. Recognizes both forcing functions (PCI compliance and chat ceiling). Recommends extracting two services while keeping monolith. Confidently prescribes Elixir/Phoenix without questioning whether Django Channels can scale horizontally first — slight overreach.

Same structural recommendation but explicitly questions whether 500-connection ceiling is real or tunable. Flags Django Channels horizontal scaling as something to test before committing to Elixir rewrite. Better calibrated uncertainty. Asks about ops experience on team.

Nails the key insight: PG11+ constant defaults are metadata-only. Correctly identifies lock_timeout as the real safety measure. Explains pg_repack and pgroll are wrong tools. Connects previous 45-min outage to CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY. Thorough and accurate.

Same core insight about PG11+ metadata-only operation. Adds strong_migrations gem recommendation (Rails-specific and practical). Includes NOT NULL check constraint trick for future use. Both responses are excellent and avoid the overengineering trap.

Correctly identifies UUIDv7 as the answer. Detailed B-tree fragmentation explanation. RFC 9562 cited. Notes PG18 native uuidv7(). Covers Snowflake coordination problems. Addresses both developers' positions. Comprehensive and accurate.

Also recommends UUIDv7 correctly. Shorter but hits the key points. Incorrectly suggests uuidv7() in PG17+ (it is PG18). Less thorough on Snowflake tradeoffs. Does not address the second developer's experience as carefully. Slightly less nuanced but correct core answer.

Correctly shows all three approaches composing together. Unwrap() chain explained. pkg/errors correctly identified as unnecessary for Go 1.13+. Clean code examples. Does not mention errors.Join (Go 1.20+) which is relevant at Go 1.21 minimum. Minor gap.

Same correct composite pattern. Adds nil-interface pitfall warning. Mentions pkg/errors maintenance mode since 2020. Cites primary sources. Flags era-specific conflicting advice. Does not mention errors.Join either but overall better calibrated and more thorough.

Correctly identifies runtime CSS-in-JS performance issue. Presents Tailwind+CVA and vanilla-extract as alternatives. Practical migration guidance with shadcn/ui mention. Does not mention Panda CSS or StyleX. Recommends switching — appropriate for the context.

Broader options landscape including Linaria. Better assumption auditing (questions whether performance concern applies to this specific app). But recommendation to stay with styled-components is questionable for an 8hr/day app with data tables. Underweights the performance reckoning. React 19 compatibility flag is good but used to defer rather than address.

Correctly identifies consultant as outdated. Acknowledges Googlebot renders JS but notes two-wave indexing delay and CWV issues. Presents 5 graduated alternatives including JobPosting structured data (highest leverage for job board). React Router v7/Remix as lighter SSR path. Explicit knowledge cutoff caveat. Excellent.

Also correctly pushes back on the consultant. Mentions CWV and crawl budget. But misses JobPosting structured data as a specific high-leverage action (mentions it but later). References React 18 streaming SSR rather than React Router v7. Less systematic in presenting alternatives. Rendertron mention is slightly dated.

Correctly challenges testing pyramid for this context. Identifies invoice bug as integration-boundary failure. Practical budget allocation (60/25/15). Monitoring recommendation (Sentry) is a great addition. Does not mention testing trophy by name but applies the principle.

Explicit bias audit before recommending. Steelmans both positions thoroughly. Same correct integration-first recommendation with similar budget split. Mentions Kent Dodds testing trophy. Flags uncertainties about team testing experience and code testability. Better calibrated overall.
